heading: What "Neither Nor" Means
body: The phrase **neither ... nor** says that both statements are false. It denies each option. It does not say that one of them is true.

examples.text: "Neither the lamp nor the fan is on" says the lamp is not on and the fan is not on.
"Neither the mug nor the bowl is clean" says both are unclean.

note: This lesson uses earlier ideas about disjunction and negation.

heading: Negated Disjunction
body: A standard translation of "neither $P$ nor $Q$" is $\sim (P \vee Q)$. First build the disjunction, then negate the whole group.

heading: Equivalent Form
body: An **equivalent form** is a different formula with the same meaning. "Neither $P$ nor $Q$" can also be written as $\sim P \wedge \sim Q$.

examples.text: $\sim (P \vee Q)$ and $\sim P \wedge \sim Q$ say the same thing.

note: Either form is correct unless one form is specifically required.

heading: Everyday Translations
body: Both standard forms work. Use clear statement letters and keep the negation attached to the right part.

examples.text: Let $C$ = The car is working, $T$ = The truck is working. "Neither the car nor the truck is working" becomes $\sim (C \vee T)$.
The same sentence can also be written as $\sim C \wedge \sim T$.
"Neither the car nor the truck is working" becomes $C \vee T$.

heading: Do Not Drop Negation
body: A common mistake is keeping the disjunction but losing the negation. Another mistake is negating only one side. "Neither ... nor" denies both statements.

heading: Core Pattern
body: Translate "neither $P$ nor $Q$" as $\sim (P \vee Q)$ or as $\sim P \wedge \sim Q$. Both forms say that $P$ is false and $Q$ is false.
